 {"id":"flywheel_connectors-bza06","title":"[airtable] Exit protocol loop after shutdown acknowledgment","description":"connectors/airtable/src/main.rs routes the `shutdown` RPC to AirtableConnector::handle_shutdown(), which returns `{"status":"shutdown"}`, but the outer stdin loop keeps reading lines forever.  {"id":"flywheel_connectors-c0fv","title":"[POL] Diff + risk summary engine","description":"# [POL] Diff + risk summary engine

## Goal
Provide deterministic diffs and risk summaries between policy bundles.

## Deliverables
- Diff engine (stable ordering) for:
  - Zone ceilings, capability grants, roles, and resource bindings.
  - Network constraints expansions (hosts/ports/CIDR changes).
  - Approval/elevation/declassification rules.
- Risk summary with reason codes and severity tiers.

## Design Notes
- Output must be stable and canonical for reproducible reviews.
- Risk summary flags widening egress, new risky caps, and lowered integrity.

## Tests
- Unit tests for diff correctness and ordering stability.
- Golden snapshot tests for representative bundle pairs.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Same inputs always yield identical diff output.
- Summary flags risky changes with clear reason codes.","notes":"[AlienArtifactPractical]
- Add evidence ledger fields with per-check log-odds contributions and explicit loss decision.
- Add anytime-valid risk control (e-process) for approvals/denials and record epsilon in receipts.
[ExtremeOptimizationPractical]
- Compile policies to DAG/BDD and cache allow/deny results with LRU.
- Use bitset capability sets for fast subset checks.

 {"id":"flywheel_connectors-c4cm","title":"[IDEA-26] Source Diversity Enforcement + Metrics","description":"## Background
Spec requires source diversity to avoid single-source reconstruction. We need mechanical enforcement and metrics to prove diversity SLOs.

## Goals
- Track per-object symbol source diversity.
- Enforce minimum distinct sources for reconstruction when policy requires.
- Emit metrics and structured logs for coverage and diversity.

## Non-goals
- Full repair controller redesign (only diversity enforcement + metrics).

## Approach
- Extend symbol store metadata to track source node IDs.
- Add policy flag for `min_source_diversity` per zone.
- Integrate with repair loop to fetch from additional sources.

## Risks
- Extra network cost; ensure policy-gated and bounded.

## Success Criteria
- Single-source prevention triggers with stable reason codes.
- Metrics expose diversity coverage per object.

## Acceptance Criteria (Refined)
- Single‑source reconstruction is denied when policy requires diversity.
- Diversity metrics are surfaced for each object.
","notes":"[AlienArtifactPractical]
- Add spectral routing score (effective resistance / Laplacian) as optional metric and log chosen path.
- Set gossip TTL from measured mixing-time; add convergence test that asserts bound.
[ExtremeOptimizationPractical]
- Use SmallVec for usually-small object/symbol lists to reduce allocations.
- Batch symbol requests and use Bytes for zero-copy in hot paths.

 {"id":"flywheel_connectors-c6i0j","title":"[REVIEW-A9-FUZZ][P2] fcp-webhook/provider.rs: missing fuzz target for per-provider signature scheme parsers (Stripe/Slack/etc.)","description":"fuzz/fuzz_targets/webhook_signature.rs covers HMAC-SHA256 verification but does NOT cover the per-provider parsers that extract the timestamp + signature(s) from provider-specific HTTP header formats BEFORE signature verification runs. Those parsers (Stripe's Stripe-Signature 't=...,v1=...' format, Slack's X-Slack-Signature 'v0=hex' + X-Slack-Request-Timestamp pair, GitHub's X-Hub-Signature-256 'sha256=hex', Linear's signature scheme, etc.) take attacker-controlled bytes and must reject malformed input cleanly.

fuzzable input shape:

#[derive(Arbitrary, Debug)]
struct WebhookHeaderFuzz<'a> {
    provider: WebhookProviderKind,
    signature_header: &'a [u8],
    timestamp_header: Option<&'a [u8]>,
    body: &'a [u8],
}

fuzz_target!(|input: WebhookHeaderFuzz| {
    let _ = parse_provider_signature_header(input.provider, input.signature_header, input.timestamp_header);
    // Property: no panic on any input, no allocation > 16 KiB
});

properties to exercise per provider:

- Stripe: 'Stripe-Signature: t=<unix>,v1=<hex>,v0=<hex>' — fuzz with duplicated keys, mixed schemes, missing t=, t with non-numeric, multi-MB hex strings (existing 'Stripe signature unbounded allocation cap' fix per MEMORY.md should hold but coverage isn't a fuzz)
- Slack: header pair with timestamp >5 min in future, signature with leading whitespace, signature with embedded NUL
- GitHub: 'sha256=' prefix tolerance to mixed case, missing prefix, double prefix
- Generic HMAC: empty signature, signature longer than expected hash output (truncation must reject, not silently accept matching prefix)
- Timestamp header with negative value, exponent notation, leading zeros, integer overflow on `now - timestamp`

priority rationale: P2. Webhook signature parsers are the SINGLE auth boundary for webhook deliveries; a parse panic kills the webhook handler thread and forces a retry. A parse confusion (e.g., reading v0 as v1) silently accepts a forged delivery. The Stripe unbounded-allocation defect was real and shipped as a fix; a fuzzer would have surfaced it earlier.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-27T02:21:44.309480Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-27T02:21:44.309480Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}

 {"id":"flywheel_connectors-ezkdk","title":"[FCP3/P3.4] Establish durable object lifecycle, repair, and garbage collection invariants","description":"Background: durable objects are only useful if their lifecycle is explicit. Mesh placement and failover depend on being able to reason about object creation, mutation, compaction, replay, repair, and garbage collection without guessing.

Scope: define invariants for object lifecycle, repair, and garbage collection across the durable plane, including when checkpoint chains are authoritative, how revoked or superseded objects remain auditable, and how repair workflows recover canonical truth after partial failure. Add deterministic lifecycle snapshots, crate local unit tests, repair integration tests, failure oriented end to end test scripts, repair transcripts, and garbage collection reason logs that later conformance and end to end harnesses can assert directly.

Acceptance: the durable plane has explicit lifecycle, repair, and garbage collection semantics that downstream mesh and operator flows can rely on, with auditable artifacts, tests, and logs that make repair and retention regressions diagnosable.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"AzureOriole","created_at":"2026-03-27T18:32:05.690966Z","created_by":"CobaltHarbor","updated_at":"2026-03-31T03:32:45.291249Z","closed_at":"2026-03-31T03:32:45.291058Z","close_reason":"Accepted: lifecycle snapshot, GC transcript, repair evaluation/report, and offline repair evidence coverage are all in place and remotely verified via rch.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["fcp3","fcp3-refoundation","gc","lifecycle","mesh-first","phase-3","repair"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"flywheel_connectors-ezkdk","depends_on_id":"flywheel_connectors-gg5sl","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-27T18:32:26.234448Z","created_by":"CobaltHarbor","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"flywheel_connectors-ezkdk","depends_on_id":"flywheel_connectors-ndoro","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-27T18:32:26.758398Z","created_by":"CobaltHarbor","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}],"comments":[{"id":2101,"issue_id":"flywheel_connectors-ezkdk","author":"AzureOriole","text":"Completed GC transcript sub-slice for durable lifecycle bead.

I now prune those entries explicitly with the new stable reason code repair.object_missing and added a regression that queues an object, deletes it from the symbol store, and verifies the queue is emptied with a Remove decision.","created_at":"2026-03-31T03:02:04Z"},{"id":2140,"issue_id":"flywheel_connectors-ezkdk","author":"NobleFern","text":"Deep review of the other-agent lifecycle/GC slices found two more concrete bugs. (1) In crates/fcp-store/src/gc.rs, collect_internal() still silently skipped foreign-zone roots even though missing roots already raised InvalidRoot and the lifecycle snapshot explicitly classifies foreign-zone roots.  {"id":"flywheel_connectors-f3xi","title":"TEST: Offline Capability Unit Tests (Availability Tracking, Pre-staging, Coverage Metrics)","description":"# TEST: Offline Capability Unit Tests

## Goal
Comprehensive unit tests for offline capability tracking from V2 spec section 21.

## Test Categories

### OfflineAccess Tests
- can_access() returns true when local_symbols >= k
- can_access() returns false when local_symbols < k
- coverage() calculates correct ratio (local_symbols / k)
- Edge cases: k=0, local_symbols=0, overflow

### OfflineCapability Tests
- Accessible map tracks multiple objects correctly
- Object removal updates capability state
- Query by zone returns correct subset
- Memory efficient for large object counts

### Pre-staging Tests
- Access pattern tracking updates correctly
- Frequent objects prioritized for pre-staging
- Pre-staging respects device storage limits
- Stale access patterns decay appropriately
- Pre-staging triggers before predicted access

### Integration with Stores
- Symbol store updates reflect in OfflineAccess
- Symbol eviction updates can_access() correctly
- Repair loop improves coverage metrics

## Golden Vectors
- Location: tests/golden/offline_capability/
- Include: OfflineAccess serialization, coverage calculations

## Logging Requirements (per 1n78.35)
All tests emit structured JSON:
{
  "timestamp": "RFC3339",
  "test": "can_access_sufficient_symbols",
  "object_id": "...",
  "local_symbols": 60,
  "k": 50,
  "expected_can_access": true,
  "result": "pass|fail"
}

## Fuzz Targets
- OfflineAccess from arbitrary symbol counts
- Pre-staging priority calculation

## Acceptance Criteria
- All can_access/coverage calculations correct
- Pre-staging improves offline availability
- Integration with stores is consistent","status":"closed","priority":0,"issue_type":"task","owner":"jeff141421@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-01-15T08:41:38.187529965Z","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone","updated_at":"2026-01-16T07:42:07.112633034Z","closed_at":"2026-01-16T07:42:07.112633034Z","close_reason":"26 comprehensive unit tests covering all requirements: OfflineAccess, OfflineCapability, AccessPatternTracker with edge cases","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["fcp2","mvp","storage","testing"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"flywheel_connectors-f3xi","depends_on_id":"flywheel_connectors-1n78.35","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-02T18:31:41Z","created_by":"import","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"flywheel_connectors-f3xi","depends_on_id":"flywheel_connectors-31c7","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-02T18:31:41Z","created_by":"import","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
 {"id":"flywheel_connectors-f46kk","title":"[REVIEW-CONVERGE][MEDIUM] fcp-streaming reconnect backoff still resets on immediate Ping/Pong","description":"P2 converge finding from commit 110dd60e. Evidence: crates/fcp-streaming/src/websocket.rs:791-809 now preserves backoff only for `message.is_close()`. WsConnection::recv at 632-640 returns Ping and Pong frames as ordinary `WsMessage`s, and poll_next at 857-860 still forwards every `Ok(Some(message))` through note_message_received. Attack/bug scenario: an adversarial peer can complete the websocket handshake and immediately send Ping or Pong control frames without ever delivering an application message; note_message_received treats those frames as healthy and resets the reconnect budget to zero, so the near-hot reconnect loop survives under a different control frame. The comment says ping/pong round-trip is healthy, but this code observes only inbound control frames, not a verified round-trip initiated by the client. Suggested fix: only reset after Text/Binary (or after an explicitly tracked outbound-ping/inbound-pong round-trip) and add regressions for immediate Ping and immediate Pong preserving backoff.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-23T22:09:05.686872Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-23T22:16:14.166712Z","closed_at":"2026-04-23T22:16:14.166378Z","close_reason":"Tightened note_message_received's reset predicate to require a data-carrying application frame (Text or Binary). The hjaej fix correctly excluded Close, but Ping and Pong control frames still reached note_message_received via Ok(Some(WsMessage::Ping/Pong(..))) from recv() and satisfied the previous '!is_close()' gate — an adversarial peer that completed the WS handshake and streamed Pings (or replayed an unsolicited Pong) would have reset the retry budget to zero, preserving the reconnect-storm hole under a different control frame. The new predicate is '!self.reset_backoff_after_first_message || !(is_text() || is_binary())' short-circuit. 3 new regression tests: reconnect_stream_ping_frame_does_not_reset_backoff (bead scenario), reconnect_stream_pong_frame_does_not_reset_backoff (symmetric; inbound Pong is not a verified round-trip at this call site), reconnect_stream_data_frame_after_control_burst_resets_backoff (Ping+Pong+Close sequence leaves gate armed; subsequent Binary frame clears the budget as expected). Verified: 7 reconnect_stream tests pass (4 pre-existing + 3 new); 525 fcp-streaming lib tests pass (was 522 + 3 new); 1 pre-existing flaky timing failure unrelated and reproduces on HEAD.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"flywheel_connectors-f4sw","title":"[IDEA-25] RaptorQ Parameter Auto-Tuning + Benchmarks","description":"## Background
Symbol size and repair parameters strongly impact performance and MTU safety. Current defaults are static and may be suboptimal per platform/path.

## Goals
- Add auto-tuning for symbol size and repair symbol count.
- Provide benchmark suite to compare configurations.
- Emit recommended parameters for common MTUs (LAN/DERP).

## Non-goals
- Changing the canonical wire format; only tune parameters.

## Approach
- Add a bench harness in `fcp-raptorq` and `fcp-cli bench` integration.
- Record decode/encode time, memory, and overhead.
- Store recommended presets in config.

## Risks
- Overfitting to benchmarks; keep conservative defaults and allow override.

## Success Criteria
- Bench suite produces consistent metrics across runs.
- Recommended parameters reduce p99 latency without MTU regressions.

## Acceptance Criteria (Refined)
- Bench suite outputs p50/p95/p99 and memory usage JSON.
- Preset table covers LAN and DERP MTU defaults.

 {"id":"flywheel_connectors-iqrb.7","title":"CLI: fcp new <name> (Connector scaffold + compliance precheck)","description":"# CLI: `fcp new <name>` (Connector scaffold + compliance precheck)

## Goal
Make it fast and safe to create a new **FCP2-compliant** connector crate by generating a correct skeleton (manifest + SDK surface + tests) and running mechanical prechecks.

## Context
We will build many connectors (Telegram, Discord, etc.). The highest leverage DX improvement is a generator that enforces:
- single-zone binding,
- default-deny NetworkConstraints,
- secretless operation by default (no secrets on disk; no logging secrets),
- structured JSON logging,
- unit test + E2E scaffolding,
- and a clear “what you must fill in” checklist.

This command is **developer tooling**. It must not weaken production security assumptions.

## Scope
In scope:
- Create a new connector crate with a minimal-but-correct FCP2 skeleton.
- Emit a default manifest with placeholder capabilities, sandbox profile, and NetworkConstraints.
- Emit placeholder operations and the correct receipts / error taxonomy wiring.
- Create deterministic unit test scaffolding and an E2E script skeleton.
- Run mechanical validation checks and print actionable diagnostics.

Out of scope:
- Any compatibility shim for legacy FCP1 protocols.
- Generating real credentials or storing secrets.

## Command UX (proposal)

### Basic usage
```bash
fcp new fcp.myservice
```

### Optional flags
- `--archetype request-response|streaming|bidirectional|polling|webhook|queue|file|database|cli|browser`
- `--zone z:project:<name>` (default: `z:project:default` or prompt)
- `--no-e2e` (if generating only unit test scaffolding)
- `--dry-run` (print planned files without writing)
- `--check` (validate an existing connector directory)

### Outputs
- Prints:
  - created file list
  - next steps
  - compliance precheck results (pass/fail with reasons)
- Supports `--json` output like the rest of `fcp-cli`.

## Skeleton contents (must generate)

### 1) Cargo crate
- Path: `connectors/<name>/`
- Library crate (or bin, depending on repo conventions) with:
  - minimal `Cargo.toml`
  - dependency on `fcp-sdk`
  - `#![forbid(unsafe_code)]`

### 2) Manifest
Generate an initial FCP2 manifest that is:
- syntactically valid TOML
- references:
  - connector id
  - version
  - zone binding (single-zone)
  - capabilities list
  - sandbox profile
  - NetworkConstraints (default deny)

### 3) Operations + schemas
- Provide placeholder operations that demonstrate:
  - typed input/output schemas
  - error taxonomy usage (FCP-XXXX)
  - receipt emission
  - tracing fields (`correlation_id`, `zone_id`, `connector_id`, `operation_id`)

### 4) Tests
- Unit test scaffold:
  - a “happy path” operation test using a mock transport
  - negative tests for missing capability / denied NetworkConstraints
  - redaction test: assert secrets are never logged

- E2E scaffold:
  - a script or harness integration that:
    - installs the connector
    - invokes an operation
    - verifies DecisionReceipt / AuditEvent shape
    - exercises default-deny failure path

## Mechanical prechecks (must run)
At creation time (and for `--check`), run:
- Manifest validation
- Connector compliance checklist (structure + fields)
- NetworkConstraints default-deny invariant
- “no secrets on disk” lint (no examples with plaintext tokens)

These checks should reuse the platform’s mechanical compliance runner where possible.

## Acceptance Criteria
- `fcp new <name>` creates a connector skeleton that builds in the workspace.
- Generated connector starts V2-only and includes no FCP1 language or compatibility shims.
- Generated manifest includes single-zone binding + default-deny NetworkConstraints.
- Generated tests include at least:
  - happy path
  - default deny
  - redaction
- `fcp new --check` produces actionable diagnostics and supports `--json`.

 {"id":"flywheel_connectors-iv4","title":"fcp.reddit: Messaging","description":"# fcp.reddit: Messaging (FCP2)

## Goal
Support Reddit inbox and direct messaging to enable:
- receiving replies/mentions
- sending messages to users (when permitted)

Messaging is privacy sensitive.

## Scope
- Fetch inbox (unread, mentions, messages) with pagination.
- Mark as read (optional; dangerous-ish).
- Send a message.

## Capability model (illustrative)
- `reddit.messages.read`
- `reddit.messages.send` (dangerous)
- Optional: `reddit.messages.manage` (mark read)

## FCP2 requirements
- Single-zone binding.
- NetworkConstraints pinned.
- PII hygiene:
  - never log message bodies or usernames
- For sends:
  - receipts + audit events include recipient hash + message id

## Deliverables
- Operation schemas:
  - `reddit.inbox.list`
  - `reddit.inbox.unread.list`
  - `reddit.message.send`
  - Optional: `reddit.inbox.mark_read`

## Test strategy (MUST)
- Mock-only tests for:
  - pagination
  - send request formation
  - redaction
  - audit/receipt on send

## Acceptance criteria
- Messaging operations are safe, auditable, and privacy-preserving.

 {"id":"flywheel_connectors-nlz4","title":"TEST: Capabilities Unit/Adversarial Tests (Token Verification, Grant Enforcement, Holder Proof)","description":"# TEST: Capabilities Unit/Adversarial Tests

## Goal
Prove that the capability system is airtight - no unauthorized operations can execute.

## Test Categories

### COSE_Sign1 Token Parsing
- [ ] Valid token parses and verifies
- [ ] Malformed CBOR rejected
- [ ] Invalid signature rejected
- [ ] Wrong issuer key rejected
- [ ] Missing protected headers rejected
- [ ] KID extraction and lookup works

### Token Field Validation
- [ ] Missing required fields rejected (jti, sub, iss_zone, iss_node, aud, iat, exp)
- [ ] Invalid field types rejected
- [ ] Time validation: expired token rejected
- [ ] Time validation: future iat beyond skew rejected
- [ ] aud mismatch rejected (wrong connector_id)
- [ ] aud_binary required for Risky/Dangerous - rejected if missing
- [ ] holder_node required for Risky/Dangerous - rejected if missing

### Grant Verification (grant_object_ids)
- [ ] Empty grant_object_ids rejected
- [ ] Referenced grant object not found → rejection
- [ ] Revoked grant object → rejection
- [ ] Token caps NOT subset of grant union → rejection
- [ ] Token caps IS subset of grant union → allow
- [ ] Attenuation that EXPANDS authority → rejection
- [ ] Attenuation that RESTRICTS authority → allow

### Checkpoint Freshness (chk_id/chk_seq)
- [ ] Missing chk_id/chk_seq rejected (for Risky/Dangerous)
- [ ] chk_id mismatch (wrong checkpoint) → rejection
- [ ] chk_seq > local checkpoint_seq → degraded mode or rejection per policy
- [ ] chk_seq <= local checkpoint_seq → allow

### Holder Proof Verification
- [ ] holder_node present but holder_proof missing → rejection
- [ ] holder_proof with wrong signable bytes → rejection
- [ ] holder_proof signed by wrong key → rejection
- [ ] holder_proof valid → allow
- [ ] Signable bytes include: request_id, operation_id, token.jti

### Issuer Verification
- [ ] Issuer node has valid NodeKeyAttestation → allow
- [ ] Issuer attestation expired → rejection
- [ ] Issuer attestation revoked → rejection
- [ ] Issuer not authorized for iss_zone → rejection

### Role Resolution
- [ ] RoleAssignment resolves to effective capabilities
- [ ] Role inheritance traverses DAG correctly
- [ ] Cyclic role inheritance detected and rejected
- [ ] Role attenuation restricts correctly

### Attack Scenarios (Adversarial)
- [ ] Token replay from different request_id rejected (via holder_proof)
- [ ] Token from revoked issuer rejected
- [ ] Token referencing revoked grant rejected
- [ ] Stale checkpoint attack: old token with old checkpoint accepted/rejected per policy
- [ ] Audience confusion: token for connector A used against connector B rejected
- [ ] Binary binding: token without aud_binary used for Dangerous op rejected

## Golden Vectors
- Valid token bytes with all fields → successful verification
- Each attack scenario → specific reason_code

## Fuzz Targets
- COSE_Sign1 parsing
- Grant subset verification
- Role inheritance resolution

## Logging Requirements
```json
{
  "test_name": "...",
  "token_jti": "...",
  "verification_step": "signature|grants|freshness|holder|issuer",
  "result": "pass|fail",
  "reason_code": "...",
  "evidence_ids": [...]
}
```

## Acceptance Criteria
- Every verification step has both positive and negative tests
- Attack scenarios are documented and tested
- Fuzz targets find no bypasses

 {"id":"flywheel_connectors-nmqqf","title":"[REVIEW-A9-CROSS][P2] connectors/openai/src/connector.rs: normalize_base_url validates shape but no host allow-list (OpenAI key routes to any https host)","description":"file: connectors/openai/src/connector.rs (normalize_base_url, ~line 730+)

repro: OpenAIConfig::from_params calls `normalize_base_url(&base_url)?` which validates URL parseability + scheme (http or https) + host presence, but does NOT validate the host is api.openai.com (or a configured-Azure-OpenAI deployment, or a known-OpenAI-compatible provider). A config with `base_url = "https://evil.example.com"` passes normalize_base_url and then constructs OpenAIClient with the OpenAI bearer token (sk-...) pointing at evil.example.com. Every chat/completion/embedding call thereafter sends the bearer to the attacker.

design tradeoff: OpenAI's ecosystem includes legitimate "OpenAI-API-compatible" endpoints — Azure OpenAI deployments (custom Azure-hosted endpoints), OpenRouter, Together AI, local LLMs (LM Studio, Ollama), self-hosted gateways. A strict allow-list of just `api.openai.com` breaks all of those. However the current "any host" stance is also wrong: a credential that the operator intended for OpenAI should not silently leak to an arbitrary attacker host.

suggested fix (defense-in-depth): require explicit operator opt-in for non-`api.openai.com` hosts via a config flag like `allow_alternative_endpoints: true` (default false). When false, normalize_base_url must reject any host other than api.openai.com (or localhost for tests). When true, the existing parseable+scheme+host check applies. Most operators want to point their OpenAI key at OpenAI; the small fraction running OpenAI-compatible providers explicitly opt in. Same pattern that the registry-trust-roots fix br-n4429 used: require explicit pinning to enable non-default trust.

Alternative (lighter): add a warning log when base_url's host is not api.openai.com so operators see in logs that their key is being sent elsewhere. No behavior change but visible audit trail.

severity: P2 because (a) operator legitimately controls the config; (b) OpenAI-compatible providers are a real use case making strict allow-list infeasible; (c) the existing scheme + host-presence checks block the simplest typos. But it is still a real defense-in-depth gap — the credential boundary is weaker than for connectors that DO pin (anthropic, datadog, zapier, etc.).

References:
- connectors/anthropic/src/connector.rs:validate_anthropic_base_url — the same shape with a strict allow-list.

WHY: F4 Failure-Mode scored revocation cache lag as RPN=240. The current gossip propagation can take 5-300 seconds depending on mesh size and connectivity.
